name: Olokun
description: Olokun is a prominent deity in the Yoruba pantheon, revered as the ruler of all bodies of water, including the ocean. Often depicted as androgynous or both male and female, Olokun embodies the unfathomable depths and mysteries of the sea. Olokun possesses immense power, controlling the tides, currents, and all marine life. As such, they are associated with both immense wealth and potential destruction. While primarily associated with the Yoruba of Nigeria, Olokun's worship has spread throughout the African diaspora, particularly in Afro-Caribbean religions like Santería and Candomblé. Olokun represents the untamed power of nature and the interconnectedness of all life. Offerings to Olokun often involve precious stones, beads, and other marine-related items, reflecting the deity's association with wealth and abundance. Olokun's devotees often seek guidance and protection from the dangers of the sea, as well as blessings of prosperity and fertility.
subtitle: Ruler of the Deep

keyMyths.title: Olokun's Jealousy
keyMyths.description: One prominent myth tells of Olokun's jealousy towards Olorun, the supreme being. Feeling overshadowed, Olokun attempted to flood the earth. Olorun, however, intervened by creating the landmasses, restraining Olokun's power and establishing a boundary between the sea and the land. This myth explains the separation of the terrestrial and aquatic realms and highlights the importance of balance within the cosmos. It also underscores the power of Olorun as the ultimate authority.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief. Longman, 1962.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Lagoons
keyMyths.description: Another myth recounts how Olokun, in a fit of rage, created the lagoons and waterways that dot the Yoruba landscape. This story illustrates Olokun's volatile nature and the unpredictable force of the ocean. These lagoons are seen as sacred spaces connected to Olokun's domain, and offerings are often made at their shores to appease the deity and ensure safe passage for those who travel upon the water. The myth emphasizes the need to respect Olokun's power.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites. Longman, 1979.

keyMyths.title: Olokun and Human Interaction
keyMyths.description: Many stories tell of Olokun interacting with humans, often through dreams or encounters at the water's edge. These interactions can bring great wealth or devastating consequences, depending on the individual's respect and understanding of Olokun's power. Some are chosen to become devotees, serving as intermediaries between the human world and the underwater realm. This myth demonstrates the constant interplay between the divine and mortal realms within Yoruba cosmology.
keyMyths.source: Olupona, Jacob K. African Religions: A Very Short Introduction. Oxford University Press, 2014.
